eq.name: Emotional Intelligence (EQ)
eq.definition: Ability to recognize, understand, and manage emotions in yourself and others.
eq.measured_by: MSCEIT, EQ-i 2.0, self-report measures, behavioral observation
eq.developable: Yes — can be improved throughout life with practice
eq.predicts: Relationship quality, leadership effectiveness, mental health, career success
eq.example: A manager notices a team member is withdrawn, checks in privately, and adjusts workload — retaining a valuable employee.

iq.name: Intelligence Quotient (IQ)
iq.definition: Measure of cognitive abilities: reasoning, problem-solving, abstract thinking, learning ability.
iq.developable: Largely stable after adolescence; education can improve crystallized intelligence
iq.predicts: Academic performance, job training speed, technical problem-solving
iq.example: An engineer quickly learns a new programming language and solves complex algorithms.

comparisons.explanation: IQ gets you in the door; EQ determines how far you go. The most effective combination is high IQ for technical competence AND high EQ for teamwork, leadership, and adaptability. A meta-analysis of 44 studies found EQ predicts job performance above and beyond IQ, with a correlation of .23 for overall performance.

self_awareness.name: Self-Awareness
self_awareness.skill: Recognizing your emotions, strengths, weaknesses, and their impact on others.
self_awareness.practice: Journaling, mindfulness, seeking feedback, the RAIN technique.
self_awareness.sign: You can name your emotions specifically and explain why you feel them.

self_regulation.name: Self-Regulation
self_regulation.skill: Managing disruptive emotions and impulses; thinking before acting.
self_regulation.practice: STOP technique, cognitive reappraisal, delayed gratification exercises.
self_regulation.sign: You respond thoughtfully instead of reacting automatically.

motivation.name: Motivation
motivation.skill: Internal drive to achieve, optimism, and commitment to goals.
motivation.practice: Connect daily tasks to larger purpose, celebrate small wins, build mastery.
motivation.sign: You persist through setbacks because the work matters to you.

empathy.name: Empathy
empathy.skill: Understanding and sharing others' emotional states.
empathy.practice: Active listening, perspective-taking exercises, reading fiction.
empathy.sign: Others feel understood and validated in your presence.

social_skills.name: Social Skills
social_skills.skill: Managing relationships, communication, conflict, and collaboration.
social_skills.practice: Assertive communication, conflict resolution, networking with genuine curiosity.
social_skills.sign: You can navigate difficult conversations and build trust quickly.

comparisons.explanation: These five components are interdependent — self-awareness enables self-regulation, which supports empathy, which informs social skills. Develop them in order: start with self-awareness (the foundation) and build outward. Most people have one or two strong components and one or two weaker ones.

suppression.name: Emotional Suppression
suppression.strategy: Pushing emotions down, pretending they don't exist.
suppression.long_term: Emotions intensify (rebound effect), physical health suffers, relationships erode.

reappraisal.name: Cognitive Reappraisal
reappraisal.strategy: Reinterpreting the situation to change its emotional impact.
reappraisal.short_term: Requires mental effort in the moment.
reappraisal.long_term: Reduces emotional intensity, improves well-being, strengthens resilience.

expression.name: Healthy Expression
expression.long_term: Builds trust, deepens relationships, prevents accumulation of resentment.

comparisons.explanation: Suppression is the least effective strategy — research by James Gross at Stanford shows it increases physiological stress and reduces memory. Reappraisal is the most effective for long-term well-being. Healthy expression combined with reappraisal provides the best outcomes. The goal is not to eliminate negative emotions but to respond to them skillfully.

keyInsight: Emotional Intelligence is not a fixed trait — it's a set of skills that can be developed at any age. The most impactful starting point is self-awareness (recognizing what you feel), followed by self-regulation (choosing how to respond). Practice the RAIN and STOP techniques to build the gap between stimulus and response.
